a really cool benefit of using React is that you don't have to know anything about the DOM, for the most part.

Instead of using `id`s, you can use the state of the react component. So when rendering this component, you should check the state to see if it's `load_binary` or not (side note: I would probably recommend changing this to `desired_load_type` with options `binary` or `process`). If it's load binary, you can render the `Load Binary` button, otherwise render the `Attach Process` button. You can create variables with the various buttons, like `let load_binary_button = ...` where `...` is valid JSX. Then you can use that with `{load_binary_button}`.

One should never have to call `document.getelementById` when using a React component.

The specific problem I've run into is that the JSON module that comes bundled with Hass.io does not throw the (somewhat) standard json.decode.JSONDecodeError on parse errors. But I think I've got the preliminary checks prior to parsing tight enough that parse errors would truly be... ahem... exceptional. I left it in, being conservative on pyvera's stability side. Now, not every best or bad practice is best or bad in every circumstance, but I agree it's overly broad now that other work mitigates much of the risk on the fetches. I'll pull it out.

That does, however, introduce a risk from a different direction. After making a successful fetch of data, updates are sent to the "observers" through callbacks, and anything, including any exception, can be raised in those callbacks outside of pyvera's control, but would take pyvera's thread down as a whole. As currently structured, the callbacks are invoked within the same try block as the data fetches, so once the current broad exception is removed, those exceptions arising in a callback will stop the loop. So, I think some additional protection around the invocation of *each* callback is warranted, again for the longevity of the loop, but the question then is, what are you comfortable with? Since a callback can do anything, this is one of those places where I would argue that the widest possible exception trap is perhaps warranted. Again, this is a very limited scope.

What are your thoughts?

Granted, `line`, `column` and `position` should always be integers, but in regards to https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/6759 / https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.6.0a2/conda/__init__.py#L48, I'd prefer every `CondaError` `message` to be a static string, e.g., either use
```python
class ConfigurationLoadError(ConfigurationError):
    def __init__(self, path, message_addition='', **kwargs):
[...]
                raise ConfigurationLoadError(filepath,
                                             "  reason: invalid yaml at line %(line)s, column %(column)s",
                                             line=mark.line, column=mark.column)
            except ReaderError as err:
                raise ConfigurationLoadError(filepath, "  reason: invalid yaml at position %(position)s",
                                                       position=err.position)
```
or
```python
class ConfigurationLoadError(ConfigurationError):
    def __init__(self, path, message_addition=''):
        message = "Unable to load configuration file.\n  path: %(path)s\n%(message_addition)s"
        super(ConfigurationLoadError, self).__init__(message, message_addition=message_addition, path=path)
```

I agree, the tuple is a holdover from python3's inspect.getargspec that I did not reevaluate. 

As this object adds more information, it might be wise to converge it to python 3's inspect.Signature object, which seems to be the [canonical future](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0362/) of signatures in python. 

Signature has an ordered dict of Parameter (name, kind, default, annotation) objects. I think this might make cleaner parsing in core._ParseFn, and I worry that without this representation your signature and python3's signature will evolve in different directions. It is also probably overpowered for what you need, and an example of small pull requests turning into larger pull requests.

To expand more on this a little. One of the main issues is this dereferenceing flattens the task graph. Take for example a temperature and image based event stream. At the end of the day you need 1/T and corrected images. These are separate processes and can be handled in their own separate nodes. However, propagating the data encourages users to depend on having the extra data in a single stream. This in turn causes reprocessing issues, if we need to reprocess the data for some reason (faulty image processing) we will also need to reperform the 1/T transformation somewhere in the stack, that where is now a sticky question because depending on where in the chain that transformation occurred either we need to propagate it a lot or a little (even more painful if instead of temperature the second data was another set of images with their own corrections)

Looking into this, I found #1823. There and in the linked blogpost, one person claims `python-tools` and `python-pip` need to be installed without really saying why. Initially, the post talks about CentOS 7 and [later](https://mangolassi.it/topic/7127/setting-up-letsencrypt-on-a-centos-7-nginx-proxy) says they are necessary to fix `InsecurePlatformWarning`. A few things:

1. The installation of these packages do not fix `InsecurePlatformWarning` per my testing.
2. `InsecurePlatformWarning` is not a fatal error.
3. The error isn't shown in normal output (unless you asked for increase verbosity) because the error is irrelevant as we're verifying the hashes of the downloaded packages.

With that said, I looked into the contents of the `python34-tools` package using `repoquery` as described [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/104055/how-to-list-the-contents-of-a-package-using-yum). This package contains:

1. [2to3](https://docs.python.org/2/library/2to3.html) and [IDLE](https://docs.python.org/2/library/idle.html) placed in the user's PATH.
2. A bunch of Python developer tools and demos that aren't placed in the user's PATH or in a location importable by Python.

I think this package is cruft and can be removed, especially in the Python 3.4 case which doesn't (currently) have `InsecurePlatformWarning` problems. I think this cleans up `certbot-auto` and reduces our dependency count a bit, but the installed package is only 1.2 MB. If you'd prefer to leave it in despite what I've found, that's fine with me.

By my reading this will change the behavior of `treq.post(url)` (with no additional arguments). The `has_json` condition will always be `True` when this shortcut method is used, so the POST request will have a payload with `Content-Type: application/json` and an entity body of `null`.

While the current default behavior of sending an empty POST request is not particularly useful, sending JSON `null` by default seems surprising. Changing the behavior is technically backwards-incompatible. I'd suggest using a sentinel object to indicate whether the `json` keyword argument is set, or simply omitting it from the `def post()` signature.

I tested this a bit and noticed that the wheel-installed files will be ignored if they are not *also* written directly to the chroot via `self._chroot.write`. The Chroot class maintains an internal set of written files (by label) in `Chroot.filesets` and files that are included in this data structure get written out to the pex zip in `Chroot.zip` . So any files installed directly by wheel that are not also installed in the normal path below will be ignored.

There doesn't appear to be a clean way to update chroot.filesets without writing directly, so perhaps the best approach here is to install the wheel into a temporary directory, then read the installed RECORD file and write each entry to the chroot?

It's a bit confusing because this function is always called "lerp," for "linear interpolation" (which is why I renamed it from the non-descriptive "remap"), but in fact it does not describe a linear transformation: the function `lambda x: lerp(x, 0, 1, 10, 11)` is the same as `lambda x: x + 10`, which is an affine transformation but not a linear one. (You can think of an affine transformation as a "linear transformation where you're also allowed to translate." In a linear transformation, you take your space, put a pin in the origin, and stretch the space; in an affine transformation, you're allowed to move the pin, too.)

I added a paragraph or two to each of the validation scripts.  Here's the short summary of goals for each of them:

• screen_plot.py: Just make plots, can confirm visually that phases are the same, but amplitudes of different modes are different.
• screen_scale.py: Investigate how the resolution of the phase screens affects FFT PSFs.
• firstKick.py: Confirm that FFT PSF and geometric first kick give similar PSFs when screens are restricted to small k, even for short exposure times.  In particular, see how large we can make kcrit before the agreement breaks down.
• firstKick_screen_scale.py:  See how the first kick PSF varies with scale of phase screens.
• secondKick.py: Confirm that FFT PSF and second kick give similar PSFs when screens are restricted to large k.  This one needs to be in the longish exposure limit.
• vonKarman.py: Compare FFT PSF using all k-modes to geometric PSF using both kicks, and also to analytic expectation value of PSF.

The basic results for the above are:

• FFT PSFs get larger with increasing resolution.  We haven't completely converged by 1cm resolution, but it's probably close enough.
• The first kick doesn't need as much resolution as FFT PSFs though, which is good since that means we can use memory to make the screens larger instead of higher resolution.  That in turn is good in that it's less likely for the screens to wrap around during the course of an exposure if they're larger.
• First kick matches expected behavior wrt FFT up to about kcrit=0.2.  I think we want the first kick contribution relatively large if possible (so a relatively large kcrit), since the second kick contribution is perfectly isotropic and we want the final PSFs to have some ellipticity.  Some more study of this is merited, which I plan to do at NERSC soon, but not before we want the second kick available for DC2 run 1.1i, I think.
• Second kick matches expected behavior wrt FFT over a wide range of kcrit.
• Full geometric PSF has size consistent with geom PSF and consistent with analytic VonKarman model over a fairly wide range in wavelength (I tested 350nm to 1100nm), r0_500 (I tested 0.05m to 0.25m) and kcrit (I looked at kcrit between 0.05 and 0.5; some of the kcrit=0.5 geometric PSFs were noticeably too large, but for kcrit <= 0.2, everything looked fine).

Actually, having just jotted down the above, I think I'll add this to a short README in this directory too.

@lukesneeringer Note that by **asserting** that only `ACCEPTING_MESSAGES` could advance, this meant that **any** commit initiated via `Batch.commit()` would bail out at this point. This is because `Batch.commit()` sets `status` to `in-flight` (which is not equal to "accepting messages").

This is the **root** cause of #4575, since `Batch.publish()` calls `Batch.commit()` when it hits the `max_messages` threshold.
